textDisplay: Been on the fence about leaving the wheel for hand-building. This finally convinced me to try a coil piece this week.
Go for it — coil building is slower but the forms you can reach by hand are worth it. Would love to see what you make.
The cone 6 test tiles are so useful. Which base glaze did you use for the layered blues at 8:00?
That base is a satin white I mix in-house — happy to share the recipe in a pinned comment if there's interest.
Watching you join those coils on such a large form is mesmerizing. How do you keep the walls from slumping at that height?
Slow and dry between coils — I let each section firm up to leather-hard before adding the next. Patience is the whole trick.
Great pace on this one, easy to follow every step of the build.
Thank you — I tried to keep the timelapse slow enough to actually see the coil joins.
How long did the full vessel take start to finish including drying?
About three weeks with drying and two firings. The building itself was maybe four studio days.
Programming my first electric kiln this weekend. Your ramp-and-hold breakdown made the controller way less scary.
So glad it helped. Start with a slow bisque and you'll build confidence fast. Good luck with the first firing.
As someone opening a small gallery next year, this honesty about margins and commissions is exactly what I needed to hear.
Wishing you the best with the opening — feel free to reach out through the studio if you have questions along the way.
Loved hearing the emerging artists talk about pricing their first show. So relatable and nerve-wracking.
They were wonderful to talk to. Pricing your own work for the first time is genuinely one of the hardest parts.
Nice conversation, not rushed — you gave each artist real space to talk.
Appreciate that. I wanted it to feel like a studio visit more than an interview.
Saved this. The raku profile is the one I always get wrong — thank you for laying out the hold times.
Raku is finicky with reduction timing. Glad the profiles help — let me know how your next firing turns out.
Question: what clay body do you fire these reactive glazes on? Mine bleed completely different on porcelain.
These are all on a speckled stoneware. Reactive glazes really do shift on porcelain — worth testing both before committing.
This full start-to-finish walkthrough is the clearest one I've found. Bookmarked for my beginners' class.
That means a lot — feel free to share it with your students. Happy to answer their questions here.
The shrinkage section explained why my handles keep cracking. I never accounted for the different drying rates.
Uneven drying is the usual culprit with handles — dampen and slow it down and they'll stop pulling away.
Really clear explanation of plasticity. Do you wedge differently for a groggier clay body?
A bit — groggy bodies need less wedging and a firmer hand. Too much and you fight the tooth of the clay.
Good pacing on this one. The wet-to-fired comparison shots really drove the shrinkage point home.
Thank you — measuring the same test bar wet and fired is the demo that finally made it click for me too.
Tried your firing-log system last month and my commission tracking is so much less chaotic now. Thank you.
Love hearing that. A simple log saves so many headaches when a client asks about a piece months later.
These iron oxide surfaces are stunning. Is that rust tone from oxidation or reduction firing?
That warm rust came out of an oxidation firing — reduction pushes the same glaze toward a darker, greener metallic.
Tried a similar iron glaze last week and it went matte brown. Any idea what I'm missing?
Matte brown usually means it's underfired or the iron is too high — try bumping the cone or cutting the oxide back a little.
Question: how much red iron oxide are you adding to the base for that surface?
That one's around eight percent red iron oxide in a glossy base — a lot, but that's what gives the broken rust look.
Thanks for the clear comparison. I've only ever fired electric and this makes me want to try a gas reduction firing.
Gas opens up a whole different palette — if you can get time on a reduction kiln, celadons and shinos are worth it.
The side-by-side of the same glaze in both kilns was so helpful. Same recipe, completely different pot.
That's the fun and the frustration of it — atmosphere changes everything. Same bucket of glaze, two different results.
Foraging local clay is on my bucket list. Did the wild New Mexico clay need much processing before it threw well?
A lot of screening and slaking to pull the rocks out, but the color was worth it. Wild clay is humbling and addictive.
Question: did you blend the wild clay with a commercial body or fire it straight?
I cut it about half and half with a stoneware body — straight, it was too short to build with, but the flecks came through beautifully.
The desert clay color is incredible. Nothing from a bag looks quite like that.
Agreed — there's something about local material that carries the landscape into the pot. That's the whole draw for me.
This sculptural stoneware series is gorgeous. The fantasy-landscape forms feel like little worlds.
Thank you — the artist built each one as a kind of imagined terrain. They're even better in person at the gallery.
Nice slow look at the surfaces. The layered glazes on the sculptural pieces are unreal.
Those took several glaze firings to build up. Patience really shows in that kind of layered surface.
Question: what luster are you using over the black stoneware? Those neon edges are wild.
A mother-of-pearl luster over a dark satin base — third firing at a low temp. It's finicky but the metallic edge is worth it.
Thanks for the quick wedging demo. My clay always has air pockets and now I see I'm rushing the spiral.
That spiral takes reps — slow down and keep your weight over it. The bubbles disappear once the motion is muscle memory.
Short and useful. Ram's head vs spiral wedging finally makes sense to me.
Use whichever your wrists like — the goal is even, bubble-free clay, not a perfect technique.
Nice clear demo, not too fast to follow along at the wedging table.
Thanks — I slowed the hands down on purpose so you could actually see the fold each time.
These modular grid-built vessels are such a cool idea. Did you build each block separately and join them?
Each module was built and firmed up on its own, then scored and joined. Keeping the grid true through drying was the hard part.
Nice pace on the build. The blocky coil forms are unlike anything else on here.
Thank you — the geometry fights the clay a little, which is exactly what makes them fun to make.
Question: how do you keep the flat panels from warping in the kiln?
Slow drying and good internal supports. Flat clay panels love to warp, so I bisque them on a dead-level shelf.
Question: my thick hollow forms keep cracking at the base. Is that always an S-crack or could it be drying?
Base cracks are usually compression from throwing plus uneven drying. Compress that base well and slow the dry and it should stop.
Made your Hatch green chile this weekend and the whole studio smelled amazing. Roasting them yourself really is worth it.
Nothing beats the smell of green chile roasting. Glad it turned out — freeze a batch while they're in season.
Thanks for the clear walkthrough on peeling and freezing. First time I've done a full Hatch batch without wasting half of it.
That steam-and-peel trick saves so much chile. You'll be set through winter now.
As a transplant to New Mexico I finally understand the red-or-green question. Great practical video.
Welcome to the state — the honest answer is always Christmas. Enjoy the chile season.
The way you talk about landscape in the glaze surface really lands. Ordered your catalog after this.
Cone 6 reduction is so unpredictable in my electric kiln — this helped me troubleshoot my last firing.
Coil-built at 30 inches is wild. How long is your drying schedule before bisque?
Been lurking your channel for a year. Finally threw my first tall form because of you.
That celadon break over the carved rim is stunning. What clay body are you using?
The Southwest wild-clay episode made me go dig at my local arroyo. Rough but rewarding.
Your studio ventilation setup convinced me to finally wear an N95 when mixing dry glaze.
Slab prep tip about compressing both sides saved my last hand-built vessel from cracking.
Watched this before my community-center workshop and my students loved the coil demo.
The unity-formula math finally clicked for me here. Thank you for slowing it down.
Gas vs electric comparison was fair and honest. Sticking with my little electric for now.
Green chile break in the middle of a ceramics channel is exactly the New Mexico energy I signed up for.
Your reactive-glaze layering gave me the courage to overlap two I'd been scared to combine.
Centering tip about locking my elbow into my hip — game changer, thank you.
Love that you show the failures too. My S-cracks make sense now.
The gallery-running episode was refreshingly real about the money side. Respect.
Bought a small vessel from Modern Edge last spring — didn't realize you had a channel too!
Please do a full glaze-chemistry series. Your test-tile grids are so clear.
The way you sketch desert landforms into vessel silhouettes — going to try this in my sketchbook.
Firing schedule breakdown helped me stop over-firing my mattes. Subscribed.
Your raku episode made me sign up for a weekend workshop. Wish me luck!
This is the most calming ceramics content on the platform. Perfect for a slow studio morning.
The blue-corn tortilla detour was unexpected and delightful. Recipe please!
Trimming that foot ring cleanly is still my nemesis — your steady-hand tip helped.
Watching your kiln-loading tetris is oddly satisfying. So efficient.
As a beginner, the plasticity-and-shrinkage explanation demystified a lot. Thank you.
Your Santa Fe adobe-church episode was gorgeous. The light in that town is unreal.
Layered lusters on dark stoneware look incredible. What cone are you re-firing to?
The community-vessel build gave me chills. Art as a group effort — love it.
Signed up for your newsletter after this. Can't wait for the museum show in October.
The way you talk about landscape in the glaze surface really lands. Ordered your catalog after this.
Cone 6 reduction is so unpredictable in my electric kiln — this helped me troubleshoot my last firing.
Coil-built at 30 inches is wild. How long is your drying schedule before bisque?
Been lurking your channel for a year. Finally threw my first tall form because of you.
